question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy why 2017 data was used to by Land and Property Services in the formation of the eligible property list for the rollout of Project Stratum by Fibrus in 2020.
answer_text: As you are aware, Project Stratum was developed to improve connectivity for premises unable to access broadband services of 30 Mbps or greater, primarily across rural areas of Northern Ireland. The Project Stratum Open Market Review (OMR) exercise was carried out through June and July 2018. In line with the EU State aid rules that govern the project, my Department compiled a list of eligible premises using a version of the Pointer dataset, provided by Land and Property Services (LPS) in June 2018. An extract of Pointer, filtered to only include premises that were “built and approved”, was then provided to infrastructure providers, to which they indicated their ability to provide broadband services. LPS subsequently used a Pointer extract from August 2018 to identify a list of all premises thought to be eligible for Project Stratum. These outputs were used as the basis of the public consultation which ran from December 2018 to January 2019, which afforded people the opportunity to comment on the accuracy of the data.My Department was able to finalise the target intervention area and launch the procurement in July 2019. The inclusion of clearly defined and eligible premises, captured at a single point in time, was an essential part of the process, as it would not have been possible to launch the procurement without a defined target intervention area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans and actions are being put in place to increase transport services to day care services.
answer_text: In February 2022, I wrote to the HSC Trusts in regards to my decision to approve the Public Health Agency (PHA) Pathway to remobilise adult day care, short breaks and transport services.  I was clear that the Pathway should be implemented with immediate effect across all programmes of care.Information regarding overall service provision is currently being collected by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) to support the remobilisation of services. As of May 2022, the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) receive fortnightly updates from each Trust on progress towards the full remobilisation of adult day care, short breaks and transport. Data across all relevant Programmes of Care is collated by SPPG to assist with the development of a regional consistent approach. Data is baselined against service provision in February 2020, which is defined as pre-pandemic capacity. Data across all Programmes of Care is analysed with the data for Learning Disability currently being published monthly on the Department of Health website.  https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/remobilisation-learning-disability-servicesThere is a continued focus in HSCTs regarding the remobilisation of transport services to day care services. SPPG will continue to engage in performance meetings with Trusts to discuss any noted barriers to full transport and day care service remobilisation and to agree mitigating actions to support service remobilisation. Furthermore, I have committed to issuing periodic communication to MLAs to outline progress made by the Trusts towards full service remobilisation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what measures are being considered to allow those who reside on roads with new broadband infrastructure, as part of Project Stratum, to join the network even if their property address is not included.
answer_text: As you are aware, Project Stratum was developed to improve connectivity for premises unable to access broadband services of 30 Megabits per second or greater, primarily across rural areas of Northern Ireland. The project has been allocated £197m of public funding, £150m of which was secured under the Confidence and Supply Agreement between the DUP and the Conservatives. Fibrus Networks was appointed as the contractor in November 2020 and is currently delivering gigabit-capable broadband to 85,000 premises in rural areas across Northern Ireland.Fibrus Networks may consider a premises to be viable for inclusion, with no public subsidy required, should, for example, the premises be close to planned new infrastructure, and there is sufficient capacity. However, the network is specifically designed to connect the premises included in the Project Stratum intervention area. A requirement of the network design is that any spare capacity is kept to a minimum. Based upon the design and build principles, whilst the fibre network may pass premises not included in the Project Stratum contract it does not necessarily mean that they could be served as there may not be any spare fibres or active networking equipment available. This would be a matter for the contractor to determine.Fibrus Networks has also developed a Self-Build/Fund product whereby if a premises is within proximity of the network but not included in Project Stratum and there is a cost to connect them, a contribution from both Fibrus Networks and the end-user can be made to allow the property to connect to the new network. If your constituents are interested in considering the Self-Build/Fund option they can their register interest by emailing details to sfsb@fibrus.com. My Department shares this option to ensure that customers are aware of all possible options; this option is, however, a matter for the individual, as take up may prevent the premises from inclusion in future interventions, such as Project Gigabit.My Department continues to explore what additional measures can be implemented in Northern Ireland under the UK Government’s Project Gigabit plan. This scheme is expected to benefit premises that are unable to access gigabit-capable broadband services and are not part of suppliers’ commercial deployment plans. My Department will be in a position to make further announcements in relation to Project Gigabit in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the obstacles that have prevented a return to full service provisions for those who attend day care.
answer_text: In February 2022, I wrote to the HSC Trusts in regards to my decision to approve the Public Health Agency (PHA) Pathway to remobilise adult day care, short breaks and transport services.  I was clear that the Pathway should be implemented with immediate effect across all programmes of care.Information regarding service provision is currently being collected by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) to support the remobilisation of services. As of May 2022, the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) receive fortnightly updates from each Trust on progress towards the full remobilisation of adult day care, short breaks and transport. Data across all relevant Programmes of Care is collated by SPPG to assist with the development of a regional consistent approach. Data is baselined against service provision in February 2020, which is defined as pre-pandemic capacity. Data across all Programmes of Care is analysed with the data for Learning Disability currently being published monthly on the Department of Health website.  https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/remobilisation-learning-disability-servicesThere is a continued focus within Trusts to remobilise day care services, however there is a cohort of service users still not receiving adult day care or receiving a partial service.  It is regretable that services have not yet been fully restored to pre pandemic levels and I sincerely apologise to those individuals, families and carers. Service remobilisation will continue to be an area of focus.A percentage of service users and carers have chosen not to return to day care for a variety of reasons. Trusts continue to engage with those  service users who have chosen not to do this to ensure their needs continue to be met through alternative service pathways, such as day opportunities whilst advising that the option of day care remains available to them. The need for HSCTs to respond to Covid-19 outbreaks has impacted on a return to full day care provision. Workforce challenges and Infection Prevention and Control considerations are obstacles cited by HSCTs towards full service remobilisation. The PHA revised guidance issued to Trusts on 28 June 2022 to clarify the position on physical distancing and management of close contacts is intended to support Trusts to balance Covid-19 risks with wider risks to the wellbeing of service users and carers, to enable the successful restoration of adult day care services. Performance management meetings led by SPPG enable any performance issues to be addressed and facilitate a focus on mitigating actions required by HSCTs to support improvement, rather than focusing on barriers to service remobilisation. SPPG will continue to engage in performance meetings with Trusts to support continued progress towards full day care service provision.Furthermore, I have committed to issuing periodic communication to MLAs to outline progress made by the Trusts towards full service remobilisation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on what additional resources are being secured to assist a return to full service provision for those adults with learning disabilities who use day care services.
answer_text: In February 2022, I wrote to the HSC Trusts in regards to my decision to approve the Public Health Agency (PHA) Pathway to remobilise adult day care, short breaks and transport services.  I was clear that the Pathway should be implemented with immediate effect across all programmes of care.Information regarding overall adult day care provision is currently being collected by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) to support the remobilisation of services. As of May 2022, the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) receive fortnightly updates from each Trust on progress towards the full remobilisation of adult day care, short breaks and transport. Data across all relevant Programmes of Care is collated by SPPG to assist with the development of a regional consistent approach. Data is baselined against service provision in February 2020, which is defined as pre-pandemic capacity. Data across all Programmes of Care is analysed with the data for Learning Disability currently being published monthly on the Department of Health website.  https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/remobilisation-learning-disability-servicesThere is a continued focus within Trusts to remobilise day care services, however there is a cohort of service users still not receiving adult day care or receiving a partial service.  It is regretable that services have not yet been fully restored to pre pandemic levels and I sincerely apologise to those individuals, families and carers. Service remobilisation will continue to be an area of focus. To date, no additional resource has been secured to support a return to full day care service provision for adults with a learning disability. A percentage of service users and carers have chosen not to return to day care for a variety of reasons. Trusts continue to engage with those  service users who have chosen not to do this to ensure their needs continue to be met through alternative service pathways, such as day opportunities whilst advising that the option of day care remains available to them. SPPG will continue to engage in performance meetings with Trusts to discuss any noted barriers to full day care service remobilisation and to agree mitigating actions or resource as appropriate, to support continued progress towards full remobilisation. Furthermore, I have committed to issuing periodic communication to MLAs to outline progress made by the Trusts towards full service remobilisation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when a full return of services in day care placements, transport services and respite care for the learning disabled will take place.
answer_text: In February 2022, I wrote to the HSC Trusts in regards to my decision to approve the Public Health Agency (PHA) Pathway to remobilise adult day care, short breaks and transport services.  I was clear that the Pathway should be implemented with immediate effect across all programmes of care.Information regarding service provision is currently being collected by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) to support the remobilisation of services. As of May 2022, the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) receive fortnightly updates from each Trust on progress towards the full remobilisation of adult day care, short breaks and transport. Data across all relevant Programmes of Care is collated by SPPG to assist with the development of a regional consistent approach. Data is baselined against service provision in February 2020, which is defined as pre-pandemic capacity. Data across all Programmes of Care is analysed with the data for Learning Disability currently being published monthly on the Department of Health website.  https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/remobilisation-learning-disability-servicesThere is a continued focus within Trusts to remobilise day care services, however there is a cohort of service users still not receiving adult day care or receiving a partial service.  It is regretable that services have not yet been fully restored to pre pandemic levels and I sincerely apologise to those individuals, families and carers. Service remobilisation will continue to be an area of focus.A percentage of service users and carers have chosen not to return to day care for a variety of reasons. Trusts continue to engage with those  service users who have chosen not to do this to ensure their needs continue to be met through alternative service pathways, such as day opportunities whilst advising that the option of day care remains available to them. Currently HSCTs are working towards full service remobilisation of day care as soon as possible, however it should be acknowledged that progress may be impacted by the need to respond to any emerging pressures regarding Covid-19. The PHA revised guidance issued to Trusts on 28 June 2022 to clarify the position on physical distancing and management of close contacts is intended to support Trusts to balance Covid-19 risks with wider risks to the wellbeing of service users and carers, to enable the successful restoration of transport and adult day care services. There is a continued focus within HSCTs to progress short breaks/respite service remobilisation. The guidance issued by PHA to Care Home Managers 27 July 2022, regarding Covid-19 isolation and testing guidance is intended to support short break/respite service provision. SPPG have addressed with HSCTs the need to scale up short breaks/respite provision to provide support to individuals, families and carers.SPPG will continue to engage in performance meetings with Trusts to discuss any noted barriers to service resump

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the plans and actions to manage a successful return to full services for those in adult day care.
answer_text: In February 2022, I wrote to the HSC Trusts in regards to my decision to approve the Public Health Agency (PHA) Pathway to remobilise adult day care, short breaks and transport services.  I was clear that the Pathway should be implemented with immediate effect across all programmes of care.Information regarding overall adult day care provision is currently being collected by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) to support the remobilisation of services. As of May 2022, the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) receive fortnightly updates from each Trust on progress towards the full remobilisation of adult day care, short breaks and transport. Data across all relevant Programmes of Care is collated by SPPG to assist with the development of a regional consistent approach. Data is baselined against service provision in February 2020, which is defined as pre-pandemic capacity. The data across all Programmes of Care is analysed with the data for Learning Disability currently being published monthly on the Department of Health website.  https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/remobilisation-learning-disability-servicesThere is a continued focus within Trusts to remobilise day care services, however there is a cohort of service users still not receiving adult day care or receiving a partial service.  It is regretable that services have not yet been fully restored to pre pandemic levels and I sincerely apologise to those individuals, families and carers. Service remobilisation will continue to be an area of focus. A percentage of service users and carers have chosen not to return to day care for a variety of reasons. Trusts continue to engage with those  service users who have chosen not to do this to ensure their needs continue to be met through alternative service pathways, such as day opportunities, whilst advising that the option of day care remains available to them. The PHA issued revised guidance to Trusts on 28 June 2022 to clarify the position on physical distancing and management of close contacts. This guidance is intended to support Trusts to balance Covid-19 risks with wider risks to the wellbeing of service users and carers, to enable transport provision and the successful restoration of adult day care services.Aligned to this process, SPPG engage in performance meetings with Trusts to discuss any noted barriers to full day care service remobilisation and to agree mitigating actions to support continued progress towards full remobilisation. Furthermore, I have committed to issuing periodic communication to MLAs to outline the progress made by Trusts towards a return to full services for those in day care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how many service users are currently registered for day care at Lisburn Assessment And Resource Centre.
answer_text: This information is not held centrally so the information was requested from South Eastern Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust.Currently the South Eastern Trust have 110 service users on the register for Lisburn Assessment and Resource Centre.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the process undertaken by Land and Property Services for public and third party notification of an adverse possession notice.
answer_text: Land Registry issues the applicant with Rulings on Title, which direct steps to be taken and proofs to be provided supporting the claim for adverse possession.  As part of the Rulings there is a requirement for the applicant to provide proof that steps have been taken to identify all interested parties and that they have served notice on those parties. Additionally, an advertisement giving details of the claim must be published in two newspapers circulating in the relevant area.  In some cases, advertisements are directed in the last known location of a title owner who is being dispossessed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether Northern Irish meat can be taken into mainland Europe by motorhomes or caravans when travelling via the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: There are no restrictions placed on meat, produced in NI, or legally placed on the market in Northern Ireland, taken to mainland Europe, for personal consumption, by motorhomes or caravans when travelling from Northern Ireland via the Republic of Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what consideration has been given to an increase in pay for serving police officers.
answer_text: It is a matter for the Justice Minister to bring forward proposals in respect of Police pay and allowances following advice from the Police Remuneration Review Body.  It is the role of my Department to review and where appropriate approve any such pay remits. As of 26 May there are no outstanding pay remits in relation to police officer pay.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline what consideration has been given to an increase in pay for serving police officers.
answer_text: My remit letter of 24 January asked the independent Police Remuneration Review Body (PRRB) to commence its annual review in respect of officers in the PSNI for 2022/23.The PRRB has now completed its oral evidence sessions with each of the stakeholders in Northern Ireland.  I will consider its report and recommendations when received.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the awarding of the National Joint Council pay award for Housing Executive staff, as agreed in February 2022.
answer_text: Housing Executive 2021/22 Pay Remits have been approved
